documentation: Curated editorial seeds for the Classical Hub. Each work entry holds a recommended recording (composer + performer + year + label) — text-search resolvable via the Phase 1 cascade matcher. We do NOT pin Tidal track ids here: those are unstable and resolved at runtime. Sources cited per entry when the choice is debatable; bias towards consensus picks (Gramophone Hall of Fame, Penguin Guide rosettes, BBC Building a Library). See D-020.

composers.name: Ludwig van Beethoven
composers.editor_note: Beethoven's late period redrew the boundary between symphony and oratorio, between sonata and meditation; his Ninth still defines the orchestral apex of Western art music.

composers.name: Johann Sebastian Bach
composers.editor_note: Bach was the summation of the Baroque: a polyphonic intelligence so total that subsequent centuries kept rediscovering rather than surpassing him.

composers.name: Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart
composers.editor_note: Mozart's clarity is deceptive: every Classical-era surface holds an emotional density that Romantic composers spent a century trying to recapture.

composers.name: Gustav Mahler
composers.editor_note: Mahler stretched the symphony to embrace the world — folk song, parody, despair, redemption — and changed forever what the form could carry.

composers.name: Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky
composers.editor_note: Tchaikovsky fused Russian melody with Western symphonic architecture; his ballets remain the standard repertoire for orchestral colour and pathos.

composers.name: Johannes Brahms
composers.editor_note: Brahms inherited Beethoven's symphonic burden and answered it with structural rigour and contrapuntal depth — the last classicist before modernism.

composers.name: Philip Glass
composers.editor_note: Glass redefined classical music's relationship with repetition, transforming minimalism from austerity into a vehicle for cinematic narrative.

composers.name: Giuseppe Verdi
composers.editor_note: Verdi turned Italian opera into a vehicle for moral and political drama, raising the genre to symphonic stature without sacrificing melody.

composers.name: Claude Debussy
composers.editor_note: Debussy dissolved harmonic gravity into colour and texture, opening the door to twentieth-century music while remaining quintessentially French.

composers.name: Dmitri Shostakovich
composers.editor_note: Shostakovich wrote symphonies as coded autobiography under Stalin, a private music made public — irony and devastation in equal measure.

composers.name: Antonio Vivaldi
composers.editor_note: Vivaldi's instrumental concerti shaped the Baroque idiom Bach would inherit — relentless invention behind the famous Four Seasons.

composers.name: George Frideric Handel
composers.editor_note: Handel turned the late Baroque into public spectacle, perfecting the oratorio in English while remaining cosmopolitan to his core.

composers.name: Arvo Pärt
composers.editor_note: Pärt's tintinnabuli style reduced post-tonal complexity to a meditative essence — sacred minimalism with no residue of pastiche.

composers.name: Maurice Ravel
composers.editor_note: Ravel's craft was orchestrational genius poured into miniature forms, rivalling Debussy's harmonic invention with his own gem-cutter precision.

composers.name: Robert Schumann
composers.editor_note: Schumann's Romanticism is a literary one — every cycle a confession, every miniature a fragment of an unwritten novel.

works.editor_note: The summit of the symphonic repertoire — the first symphony to incorporate voices, ending in the universal address of Schiller's Ode to Joy.

editors_choice.label: Deutsche Grammophon
editors_choice.note: The reference Beethoven cycle by critical consensus; Gramophone Hall of Fame, Penguin Guide rosette.

works.editor_note: The four-note motif that opens Beethoven's Fifth has become the Western world's shorthand for fate itself.

editors_choice.label: Deutsche Grammophon
editors_choice.note: Considered by many critics the greatest symphonic recording ever made; BBC Building a Library top choice.

works.title_canonical: Symphony No. 3 in E flat major
works.editor_note: The 'Eroica' broke open the Classical symphony — Beethoven's expansion of scale and emotional reach reshaped the genre overnight.

editors_choice.label: Archiv
editors_choice.note: Period-instrument reference; Penguin Guide rosette.

works.editor_note: The 'Moonlight' Sonata's opening Adagio is the most-played piece of late-eighteenth-century piano music, masking the volcanic finale that follows.

editors_choice.label: Deutsche Grammophon
editors_choice.note: Kempff's Beethoven cycle remains a touchstone of patrician interpretation.

works.editor_note: The 'Emperor' is Beethoven's grandest concerto, written under the Napoleonic occupation of Vienna — heroic music answering imperial trauma.

editors_choice.label: Sony Classical
editors_choice.note: Perahia's Beethoven concertos are widely regarded as a modern reference.

works.editor_note: Beethoven's most ambitious sacred work — written 'from the heart, may it return to the heart' — and one of the most demanding choral scores in the canon.

editors_choice.label: EMI
editors_choice.note: Klemperer's monumental architecture remains the philosophical reference.

works.editor_note: Bach's monumental aria with thirty variations — written, by tradition, to soothe an insomniac count, and a peak of contrapuntal imagination.

editors_choice.label: Sony Classical
editors_choice.note: Gould's second recording: slower, more meditative, his testament reading. Gramophone Hall of Fame.

works.editor_note: Bach's encyclopaedic summation of the Latin Ordinary, drawing on a lifetime of vocal writing — never performed in his lifetime.

editors_choice.label: Harmonia Mundi
editors_choice.note: Period-instrument reference; Penguin Guide rosette.

works.editor_note: Bach's six Brandenburg concertos showcase every Baroque instrumental combination he could imagine — a compendium of the genre.

editors_choice.label: Archiv
editors_choice.note: Standard period-instrument reference for decades.

works.editor_note: The summit of Lutheran sacred music — three hours of overlapping double choruses, recitative, and the most intimate aria writing in Bach's output.

editors_choice.label: Archiv
editors_choice.note: Gardiner's St Matthew is widely regarded as a benchmark; Penguin Guide rosette.

works.editor_note: Six unaccompanied cello suites built on dance forms — Bach's exploration of how much polyphony a single melodic line can imply.

editors_choice.label: EMI
editors_choice.note: The recording that brought the Suites back into the repertoire after centuries of neglect; foundational.

works.editor_note: Two books of preludes and fugues in every major and minor key — the pianist's Old Testament, the New being Beethoven's sonatas.

editors_choice.label: ECM New Series
editors_choice.note: Schiff's WTC is widely regarded as the most lucid modern reading.

works.editor_note: Mozart's last work, left incomplete at his death — its dark monumental tone unlike anything else in his output.

editors_choice.label: Philips
editors_choice.note: Gardiner's HIP reading uses the Maunder completion and is widely cited as a benchmark.

works.editor_note: Mozart's most morally complex opera — a Don Juan tragedy that ends in damnation, with arias of staggering psychological depth.

editors_choice.label: EMI
editors_choice.note: Cast featuring Eberhard Wächter, Joan Sutherland, Elisabeth Schwarzkopf — generally regarded as the studio reference.

works.editor_note: The 'Jupiter' is Mozart's farewell to the symphony — a finale combining five contrapuntal subjects in a fugal coda of unprecedented density.

editors_choice.label: Deutsche Grammophon
editors_choice.note: Böhm's late-Mozart cycle is a paragon of Classical-period interpretation.

works.title_canonical: Piano Concerto No. 21 in C major
works.editor_note: The 'Elvira Madigan' Andante made this concerto famous in the 1960s — but the surrounding allegros are equally a peak of Mozart's concerto craft.

editors_choice.label: Sony Classical
editors_choice.note: Perahia's complete Mozart concertos cycle is a critical reference.

works.editor_note: Mozart's first collaboration with Da Ponte — a comedy of class, gender, and forgiveness, with some of the most beautiful ensembles in opera.

editors_choice.label: EMI
editors_choice.note: Long considered the finest Figaro on disc; Penguin Guide rosette.

works.editor_note: Mahler's farewell symphony — a meditation on departure, written in the shadow of his own diagnosed heart disease.

editors_choice.label: Deutsche Grammophon
editors_choice.note: The Bernstein/Berlin live recording is widely cited as the reference Mahler 9; Gramophone Hall of Fame.

works.editor_note: The 'Resurrection' Symphony's choral finale is one of the great affirmations in symphonic literature, answering its first movement's funeral march.

editors_choice.label: Deutsche Grammophon
editors_choice.note: Bernstein's late Mahler cycle is the modern reference; multiple Gramophone awards.

works.title_canonical: Symphony No. 5 in C sharp minor
works.editor_note: The Adagietto for harp and strings — heard at Visconti's funeral and in countless films — is the heart of Mahler's most-played symphony.

editors_choice.label: Deutsche Grammophon
editors_choice.note: Bernstein's late Vienna recordings of Mahler are a critical benchmark.

works.editor_note: The 'Pathétique' was Tchaikovsky's last symphony, premiered nine days before his death — a deliberate reversal of symphonic catharsis ending in despair.

editors_choice.label: Deutsche Grammophon
editors_choice.note: Mravinsky's Leningrad recordings remain the Russian reference.

works.title_canonical: Piano Concerto No. 1 in B flat minor
works.editor_note: The opening unison piano chord is one of the most recognised gestures in nineteenth-century music — the rest of the concerto rivals it.

editors_choice.label: RCA
editors_choice.note: Cliburn's recording after winning the first Tchaikovsky Competition in Moscow — a Cold War cultural milestone.

works.editor_note: Initially declared 'unplayable', Tchaikovsky's Violin Concerto is now a centerpiece of the romantic concerto repertoire.

editors_choice.label: Melodiya
editors_choice.note: Oistrakh's authoritative Russian reading is the historic reference.

works.editor_note: Brahms's last symphony — the finale's passacaglia (a Baroque form revived in the late nineteenth century) crowns his classicising achievement.

editors_choice.label: Deutsche Grammophon
editors_choice.note: Kleiber's only Brahms recording — Gramophone Hall of Fame, Penguin Guide rosette.

works.editor_note: Brahms set German biblical texts rather than the Latin Mass — a humanist consolation rather than a liturgical Requiem.

editors_choice.label: EMI
editors_choice.note: The Klemperer recording is the historic reference — slow, weighted, monumental.

works.editor_note: Brahms wrote his Violin Concerto for Joseph Joachim — its slow movement opens with one of the most beloved oboe solos in the repertoire.

editors_choice.label: EMI
editors_choice.note: Oistrakh / Szell remain the critical reference for the Brahms VC.

works.title_canonical: Piano Concerto No. 2 in B flat major
works.editor_note: Symphony with piano obbligato, Brahms quipped — a four-movement concerto with the scale and weight of a symphony.

editors_choice.label: Deutsche Grammophon
editors_choice.note: Gilels's Brahms 2 is widely regarded as the most magisterial reading on disc.

works.editor_note: Glass's most accessible work — six pieces conceived as an introduction to his style, with the indelible opening 'Opening' for solo piano.

editors_choice.label: CBS
editors_choice.note: Composer's own ensemble recording remains the authoritative reading.

works.editor_note: The third in Glass's portrait-trilogy of revolutionaries (Einstein on the Beach, Satyagraha, Akhnaten) — a meditation on monotheism and exile.

editors_choice.label: CBS Masterworks
editors_choice.note: First commercial recording, supervised by the composer.

works.editor_note: Glass's most performed concert work — minimalist textures supporting a soloist of unusual lyrical scope.

editors_choice.label: Deutsche Grammophon
editors_choice.note: Kremer's advocacy brought the Glass concerto into the mainstream Romantic-concerto orbit.

works.editor_note: Verdi's late masterpiece on Boito's libretto — Shakespeare distilled into the most concentrated tragedy in opera.

editors_choice.performer: La Scala · Plácido Domingo · Mirella Freni
editors_choice.label: Deutsche Grammophon
editors_choice.note: Kleiber's Otello is widely regarded as the studio reference.

works.editor_note: Verdi's intimate domestic tragedy — Violetta's vocal line moves from coloratura giddiness to deathbed simplicity in three acts.

editors_choice.performer: Bavarian State Opera · Ileana Cotrubaș · Plácido Domingo
editors_choice.label: Deutsche Grammophon
editors_choice.note: Kleiber's Traviata is the modern studio reference; multiple critic surveys put it first.

works.editor_note: Verdi's setting of the Requiem Mass after Manzoni's death — operatic in scale and intensity, sacred in conviction.

editors_choice.label: EMI
editors_choice.note: Giulini's Verdi Requiem is the long-standing critical reference.

works.editor_note: Three symphonic sketches that reinvented orchestral writing — colour and motion replacing development as structural principles.

editors_choice.label: Deutsche Grammophon
editors_choice.note: Boulez's analytical clarity in Cleveland is the standard reference for late Debussy.

works.editor_note: The piece historians often cite as opening twentieth-century music — eight minutes that broke nineteenth-century harmony.

editors_choice.label: Deutsche Grammophon
editors_choice.note: Boulez/Cleveland Debussy edition; benchmark recording for orchestral Debussy.

works.editor_note: Composed during Shostakovich's first denunciation by Soviet authorities — a public 'rehabilitation' that hides ironic and bitter undertones.

editors_choice.label: Teldec
editors_choice.note: Rostropovich's late Shostakovich cycle is regarded as the most testimonial reading.

works.editor_note: The 'Leningrad' Symphony — composed during the siege of Leningrad — became a wartime symbol broadcast worldwide.

editors_choice.label: Deutsche Grammophon
editors_choice.note: Bernstein/CSO Leningrad: searing studio recording, Gramophone Award winner.

works.title_canonical: String Quartet No. 8 in C minor
works.editor_note: Shostakovich's most personal quartet — written 'in memory of the victims of fascism and war', signed in his musical monogram DSCH.

editors_choice.label: Melodiya
editors_choice.note: The Borodin Quartet's complete Shostakovich is a foundational document.

works.editor_note: Vivaldi's four violin concertos depicting the seasons — possibly the best-known piece of Baroque music, founding the programme-music tradition.

editors_choice.label: Opus 111
editors_choice.note: Biondi's HIP Four Seasons remade the modern conception of the work.

works.editor_note: The most-performed oratorio in the English-speaking world — Handel composed it in 24 days, and its reception in Dublin (1742) defined the genre.

editors_choice.label: L'Oiseau-Lyre
editors_choice.note: Hogwood/AAM was the first major HIP Messiah; remains a standard reference.

works.editor_note: Handel's outdoor suites for King George I's barge party on the Thames — pure Baroque sociability for orchestra.

editors_choice.label: Philips
editors_choice.note: Gardiner's HIP Water Music is the long-standing critical reference.

works.editor_note: Pärt's double concerto for two violins, prepared piano and strings — a sonic embodiment of the 'blank slate' aesthetic of his tintinnabuli style.

editors_choice.label: ECM New Series
editors_choice.note: Kremer's premiere recording on ECM defined contemporary Pärt reception.

works.editor_note: Ravel's relentless C major crescendo over a single theme — described by the composer as 'orchestration without music', yet adopted as a universal hit.

editors_choice.label: RCA
editors_choice.note: Munch/Boston's Boléro remains the orchestral-colour reference.

works.editor_note: Ravel's jazz-inflected concerto, written for himself but premiered by Marguerite Long after his health failed — the slow movement is one of his most personal pages.

editors_choice.label: Deutsche Grammophon
editors_choice.note: Argerich's Ravel concerto is the modern reference; Penguin Guide rosette.

works.editor_note: Schumann's only completed concerto — written for his wife Clara, the most celebrated pianist of the era — and a model of intimate Romantic concerto writing.

editors_choice.label: Sony Classical
editors_choice.note: Perahia's Schumann is a critical reference; Gramophone Award winner.

works.editor_note: Thirteen miniatures depicting scenes from childhood — among them 'Träumerei', perhaps the most-played encore in piano literature.

editors_choice.label: Sony Classical
editors_choice.note: Horowitz's Kinderszenen is widely cited as a benchmark, especially the Carnegie Hall live recording.
